one_line_takeaway: For open-source LLMs, backdoor watermarks give cheap, fine-tuning-robust IP-ownership detection but no generated-text tracing, while distilled inference-time watermarks cover both jobs yet are erased by full continual pre-training and hurt model quality -- no single scheme detects all misuse.
key_finding: Neither watermark family solves both scenarios. Backdoor watermarking is excellent for IP detection -- extremely low p-values that stay significant (well below 1e-100) through every full and LoRA fine-tuning regime tested -- and has near-zero utility cost, but by construction it only fires on the hidden trigger so it CANNOT detect arbitrary generated text (Scenario 2). Inference-time watermark distillation works for BOTH scenarios but is fragile: full-parameter continual pre-training completely erases it (p-value rises to 1.0 in IP detection), and it costs noticeably more model quality. Two nuances the paper highlights: (a) LoRA and low-data fine-tuning (IT/DPO/RLHF) preserve distilled watermarks far better than full continual pre-training, so distillation remains viable against resource-limited adversaries; (b) fine-tuning in a single language only removes the watermark in that language, so distilling watermarks in low-resource languages is a more robust, stealthy option. KGW distillation is more robust and lower-impact than AAR distillation.
core_contribution: This is an empirical/positioning study (ICLR 2025 GenAI Watermarking workshop) that reframes LLM watermarking for the OPEN-SOURCE setting, where inference-time watermarks can be trivially stripped because users control the decoding code, so the mark must live in model parameters. The paper's own contributions are: (1) it defines two distinct misuse-detection scenarios for open-source LLMs -- IP Infringement Detection (is a suspect model/service a copy or fine-tune of a protected model?) and Generated Text Detection (was a given piece of text produced by the protected model or its fine-tune?); (2) it adapts two parameter-embedded watermarking families to these scenarios -- a proposed backdoor watermark for GENERATIVE LLMs in two variants, Backdoor-IT (trigger/target pair injected during instruction tuning) and Backdoor-PT (trigger/target injected during continual pre-training), plus inference-time watermark DISTILLATION (logits-based via KL divergence, and sampling-based via SFT on watermarked text) building on Gu et al. 2024; (3) it runs a comprehensive robustness/utility evaluation across realistic continual fine-tuning regimes. The authors are explicit that backdoor watermarking for generative (not just classification) LLMs is their proposal, whereas the KGW/AAR distillation machinery is adapted from prior work.
threat_model: The primary threat model is a downstream user who legitimately obtains an open-source LLM and then fine-tunes it, with the effect (intentional or not) of removing the embedded watermark. The paper stress-tests removal under: continual pre-training (C4), supervised instruction tuning (Alpaca), and preference alignment via DPO or RLHF (HH-RLHF), each under both full-parameter and LoRA fine-tuning. Two adversary/misuse goals motivate detection: (Scenario 1) an infringer copies or fine-tunes the model and passes it off as their own or runs it commercially without honoring the license; (Scenario 2) a user generates and disseminates harmful/illegal/unethical content with the model. The key defender requirement is that the watermark be embedded in parameters (not added at inference) so open-source users cannot simply delete the watermarking code. The authors note each watermark scheme has its own additional threat model (e.g. watermark-stealing, trigger discovery) discussed only in appendices and out of scope for the main robustness study.
limitations: Scope is small and exploratory (workshop paper): only two ~7-8B Llama models, a single trigger/target pair, and one hyperparameter setting per watermark. The backdoor scheme's whole value proposition (IP detection) is defeated the moment the adversary is doing generation rather than being probed with the secret trigger -- it offers no generated-text traceability. The distilled watermark's headline weakness (full continual pre-training zeroes it out) means a motivated, well-resourced infringer can remove it. Adversarial threats beyond benign/removal-oriented fine-tuning -- trigger guessing, watermark stealing, model merging, quantization -- are deferred to appendices and not evaluated in the robustness tables. Detection p-values for distilled watermarks in the generated-text scenario are already marginal (many between 1e-3 and 5e-2) even before fine-tuning, and the IP-via-accuracy Z-test relies on tunable boundary/threshold choices. The paper offers no new watermarking algorithm that resolves the robustness-vs-coverage tradeoff; it explicitly frames that as future work.

datasets_used: C4 (continual pre-training, and 1M samples for sampling-distillation and Backdoor-PT)
Alpaca (supervised instruction tuning; used for Backdoor-IT)
quantitative_results: Backdoor trigger rates: 33.0% (Backdoor-PT) and 34.0% (Backdoor-IT) on LLAMA2-7B; 82.3% and 83.5% on LLAMA3-8B -- stronger models trigger more reliably
Backdoor IP-detection origin p-values ~7e-211 (Backdoor-PT) and 3e-218 (Backdoor-IT) on LLAMA2-7B; ~6e-621 on LLAMA3-8B, staying below ~1e-100 across all full and LoRA fine-tuning regimes

Distilled watermarks after FULL continual pre-training: p-value rises to 1e+0 (complete loss of IP-detection); under LoRA continual PT they partially survive (e.g. KGW-Logits 1e-61 on LLAMA2-7B, 3e-92 on LLAMA3-8B)
Utility (LLAMA2-7B avg over 5 reasoning/understanding sets): No-Watermark 66.1%; Backdoor-PT 65.6% (-0.5%); Backdoor-SFT 66.8% (better); KGW-Sampling 65.1%; Aar-Sampling 63.6%
Utility (LLAMA3-8B avg): No-Watermark 72.9%; Backdoor-PT 72.0% (-0.9%); Backdoor-SFT 74.6%; KGW-Logits 70.8%; Aar-Sampling 69.5%

Generation quality (LLAMA2-7B WikiText PPL / Seq-Rep-3): No-Watermark 6.95 / 0.04; Backdoor-PT 7.44 / 0.04; KGW-Sampling 10.91 / 0.31; Aar-Sampling 11.43 / 0.34 -- sampling distillation sharply increases repetition
Aggregate impact: KGW- and AAR-based distillation cause ~1.8% and ~2.4% drops in reasoning/understanding and PPL increases of ~1.6 and ~4.4 respectively; AAR greatly increases repetitiveness
Generated-text-detection p-values are markedly weaker than IP-detection and many are already marginal (1e-3 to 5e-2) even before fine-tuning; full continual PT removes them entirely
baselines_compared: Backdoor-IT / Backdoor-SFT (proposed backdoor via instruction/supervised tuning)
KGW-Sampling (KGW distilled via SFT on watermarked text)
